messages.content:
The original challenge was restricted to equational laws which involve at most four usages of the magma operation, which leads to 4694 distinct laws (after normalization) and a data set of 22 million implications which were completely worked out by the ETP. However, as the cheatsheets are working better than expected, we are considering expanding the Stage 1 challenge to also include some longer laws. Here we do not have a complete table of implications, but we will try to generate some data sets.

One collection of such implications can be found at https://github.com/vlad902/equational_theories/tree/order5/equational_theories/Generated/Order5 . For instance, the first implication in https://github.com/vlad902/equational_theories/blob/order5/equational_theories/Generated/Order5/MiscImplications.lean asserts that Equation38609 implies Equation26291. The equation numbering system for order 5 laws can be found at https://github.com/vlad902/equational_theories/blob/order5/data/eq_size5.txt

There are still gaps our knowledge here: for instance, in https://teorth.github.io/equational_theories/blueprint/order-5-austin-laws.html, while all the laws in Table 20.1 are known to not imply the singleton law `x=y`, this is not known for the laws in Table 20.2 or 20.3 (with the exception of law 17260).

We hope to generate some more data sets with longer equations in the near future.

If somebody wants to try, I've derived a new hard5 community dataset based on Mr. Tao msg. 

It's a a balanced 654-row order-5 benchmark for equational implication over magmas, built from current Lean proof artifacts plus Austin-law negatives. Intended as a harder follow-up to the existing SAIR hard sets.

https://gist.github.com/stokasz/d00818b4039ca55324d96d4bc4b2c3ea

@_**stokarz|50** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/Longer.20equations/near/793):
```quote
If somebody wants to try, I've derived a new hard5 community dataset based on Mr. Tao msg. 

It's a a balanced 654-row order-5 benchmark for equational implication over magmas, built from current Lean proof artifacts plus Austin-law negatives. Intended as a harder follow-up to the existing SAIR hard sets.

https://gist.github.com/stokasz/d00818b4039ca55324d96d4bc4b2c3ea
```

Awesome contribution @**stokarz** ! Testing on it now. How does your previous best performing hard cheatsheet perform on this new set?

@_**Heath Sanchez|71** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/Longer.20equations/near/794):
````quote
@_**stokarz|50** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/Longer.20equations/near/793):

https://gist.github.com/stokasz/d00818b4039ca55324d96d4bc4b2c3ea
```

Awesome contribution @**stokarz** ! Testing on it now. How does your previous best performing hard cheatsheet perform on this new set?
````

Badly. But I was able to saturate this dataset too, on GPT120b using, this time, algebraic cheatsheet. What I keep stumbling upon is a generally different distributions of problems on various datasets. And it's annoying, because my cheatsheets do not generalize well. What I am working on right now is creating a much more distributed dataset.
